statement: Bændasamtök Íslands (Icelandic Farmers' Association, BÍ) has consistently expressed serious concerns about the impact of EU membership on Icelandic agriculture. BÍ's stated position is that while it does not oppose a referendum per se, it considers EU membership itself to pose existential risks to Icelandic farming. Key concerns include: the removal of tariff protection under the EU customs union would expose Icelandic farmers to competition from larger, lower-cost EU producers; the transition from Iceland's national support system to CAP may not adequately compensate for Iceland's high production costs (sub-Arctic climate, small scale, geographic isolation); food safety and animal health standards could be compromised (Iceland has been largely free of diseases common in continental European livestock). BÍ has also called attention to what it describes as 'upplýsingaóreiða' (information disorder/misinformation) in the EU debate, arguing that some pro-EU voices understate the challenges for agriculture.
source_name: Bændasamtök Íslands — Official statements and press releases
caveats: BÍ is an advocacy organisation representing farmers' interests and its position reflects those interests. Some economists and pro-EU commentators argue that BÍ overstates the risks and understates the potential benefits of CAP participation (direct payments, rural development funding, access to EU food markets). The impact on Icelandic agriculture would depend heavily on transition arrangements negotiated during accession — Finland and Sweden's experience suggests that Arctic/sub-Arctic supplements are possible.

related_entries.statement: Iceland maintains some of the highest agricultural tariffs among OECD countries. The OECD's Producer Support Estimate (PSE) for Iceland was approximately 55–60% of gross farm receipts in 2022–2023, making it one of the highest in the OECD (compared to the EU average of approximately 18–20%). Applied tariff rates on agricultural products average 40–60%, with individual tariffs exceeding 100% on dairy products, meat, and some vegetables. Iceland's agricultural sector received an estimated ISK 20–25 billion annually (approximately EUR 130–160 million) through a combination of direct payments to farmers and border protection. Agriculture accounts for approximately 1% of Iceland's GDP but employs about 2.5–3% of the workforce, with approximately 3,500 farms primarily focused on sheep, dairy, and cattle. EU membership would require dismantling these tariff barriers (as part of the customs union) and replacing Iceland's national support framework with CAP — the fundamental restructuring that has been the primary concern of Bændasamtök Íslands (Icelandic Farmers' Association).

No EU member state has ever received a permanent exemption (opt-out) from the Common Agricultural Policy. All member states participate fully in both CAP pillars. While transition periods are routinely negotiated during accession — typically 7–10 years for full phasing-in of direct payments, and shorter periods for specific market regulations — all new member states are required to fully adopt and implement CAP rules within the agreed timeframe. Finland and Sweden (1995 accession) received authorisations for supplementary national support for Arctic/northern farming under the accession treaty, but these are additions to the standard CAP framework, not exemptions from it. The principle of no permanent opt-outs for new member states was reinforced by the 2020 revised enlargement methodology. Commissioner for Enlargement Stefan Füle (2010–2014) consistently stated that the acquis must be adopted in full by all new members.

All countries that joined the EU since 2004 received transition periods for phasing in Common Agricultural Policy direct payments. New member states started at 25% of the EU-15 payment level in their first year and reached parity over 10 years: the 2004 accession states (Poland, Czech Republic, Hungary, etc.) reached full payment parity by 2013; Bulgaria and Romania (2007) by 2016; Croatia (2013) by 2022. New member states were immediately eligible for rural development funding (Pillar II) and market support mechanisms upon accession. Additional product-specific transitions were negotiated: Poland and most 2004 states received 7–12 year transition periods on foreign purchase of agricultural land; several states had transition periods for sugar and dairy quota compliance (before quotas were abolished EU-wide in 2015 and 2017 respectively). Finland, Sweden, and Austria (1995 accession) received authorisation for supplementary national aid for Arctic/northern/mountain farming that continues alongside standard CAP payments.

citations.report_title: 25. þáttur Silfursins: Stefnt að kosningu um ESB í sumar

claims.claim_text: Bændasamtökin sögðu í dag að núverandi stefna ESB henti ekki íslenskum landbúnaði.

citations.report_title: 390.000 hektarar af landbúnaðarlandi breytast í skóg og votlendi

claims.claim_text: Ef Ísland færi í aðildarviðræður væri eðlilegt að landbúnaður yrði eitt af lykilmálunum.

citations.report_title: Ekki víst hvernig Evrópusambandið bregst við

claims.claim_text: Hugtakið upplýsingaóreiða hefur verið notað um umræðuna og sjónir beinast í auknum mæli að stjórnvöldum í þeim efnum.

citations.report_title: ESB aðild hefði veruleg neikvæð áhrif á hagsmuni íslenskra bænda

claims.claim_text: Bændasamtökin eru ekki á móti því að þjóðin fái að taka ákvörðun um hvort halda eigi áfram aðildarviðræðum við Evrópusambandið.
Samtöl við bændur og samtök þeirra í ESB-löndum sýna óánægju með málefni landbúnaðarins innan sambandsins.
Bændasamtökin hafa ekki komið auga á nein sérstök tækifæri fyrir heildarhagsmuni ísleńsks landbúnaðar með ESB-aðild.
Samkeppnisstaða íslenskra bænda er skökk gagnvart stóru matvælaframleiðslulöndunum innan ESB.
Galopnun landamæranna á innflutningi á landbúnaðarafurðum mun að öllum líkindum hafa veruleg neikvæð áhrif á hagsmuni íslenskra bænda.
Bændasamtökin munu taka virkan þátt í samtali við stjórnvöld og almenning um afleiðingar ESB-aðildar fyrir ísleńskan landbúnað ef af viðræðum verður.

citations.report_title: Ræða þarf alla kaflana á nýjan leik

claims.claim_text: Ráðherra landbúnaðarmála var ekki tilbúinn að víkja frá banni við innflutningi á lifandi dýrum í aðildarviðræðunum.
Meðal varnarlína Íslands í landbúnaðarmálum voru kröfur um að tollvernd yrði haldið áfram.
Á tíma aðildarviðræðnanna var stjórnsýsla landbúnaðarmála á Íslandi að miklum hluta í höndum Bændasamtakanna, sem er ekki í samræmi við reglur ESB.
Mikill munur var á stjórnsýslu landbúnaðarmála á Íslandi og kröfum ESB, bæði hvað varðar stefnu og framkvæmd.

citations.report_title: Snorri segir húsnæði verða jafndýrt þótt vextir lækki

claims.claim_text: Snorri segir að aðrir hagsmunir gildi í Evrópusambandinu og að Ísland yrði bara að verða fyrir skaðanum, þótt margir kostir séu við EES-samninginn.

citations.report_title: Þrír hópar sem myndu hagnast mest á aðild Íslands að Evrópusambandinu

claims.claim_text: Ótti varðandi afdrif sjávarútvegs og landbúnaðar innan ESB er uppspretta mjög umfangsmikilsm hræðsluáróðurs andstæðinga aðildar.
